textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin ': H 4.5 mmol/l (Ref: <3.0)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol level is 4.5 mmol/L, which is significantly higher than the recommended level of less than 3.0 mmol/L. High LDL cholesterol is a major risk factor for heart disease and stroke.

textInfo.title: Highly Elevated Lipoprotein(a)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Lipoprotein (a) H 257 nmol/l (<75)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Lipoprotein(a) level is 257 nmol/L, which is significantly elevated compared to the reference range of less than 75 nmol/L. High Lipoprotein(a) is a genetic risk factor for cardiovascular disease, such as heart attacks and strokes. A clinical follow-up is recommended to discuss this result.

textInfo.title: Degenerative changes and disc protrusions in the neck
textInfo.medicalDescription: Osteochondrose und Unkovertebralarthrosen sowie Diskusprotrusionen C3-7.
textInfo.originalDescription: Osteochondrose und Unkovertebralarthrosen sowie Diskusprotrusionen C3-7.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI of your neck shows signs of wear and tear in the vertebrae and discs from the third to the seventh cervical level (C3-C7). This includes osteochondrosis, which is a degenerative change to the bone and cartilage, as well as arthritis in the small joints on the sides of your vertebrae, known as uncovertebral arthroses. Furthermore, there are disc protrusions, meaning the discs between these vertebrae are bulging outwards from their normal position.

textInfo.title: hs-CRP Indicates Medium Cardiovascular Risk
textInfo.medicalDescription: CRP sensitiv: 2.4 mg/l (Ref: mittleres KHK Risiko: 1.0-3.0 mg/l). This value indicates a medium risk for coronary heart disease.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) level is 2.4 mg/L. This falls into the medium-risk category (1.0-3.0 mg/L) for developing cardiovascular disease, suggesting a moderate level of inflammation in your body.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Abdominal Fat
textInfo.medicalDescription: Total abdominal fat volume measured at 10.4 L, which is above the normal range of 8.97 - 10.08 L.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total abdominal fat volume is 10.4 liters, which is slightly above the optimal range of 8.97 to 10.08 liters. Elevated abdominal fat is associated with increased health risks, and maintaining it within a healthy range is beneficial.

textInfo.title: Mildly Reduced Kidney Function (eGFR)
textInfo.medicalDescription: eGFR (Kreatinin, CKD-EPI): L 77 ml/min/1.73m2 (Ref: >90). This value corresponds to Stage 2 Chronic Kidney Disease (mildly decreased GFR).
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) is 77 ml/min/1.73m², which is lower than the normal value of over 90. This result suggests a mild reduction in kidney function, classified as stage 2 chronic kidney disease. It's important to monitor this and manage any related health conditions.

textInfo.title: Focal cartilage defect in the right knee
textInfo.medicalDescription: Knie rechts: Fokaler umschriebener Knorpeldefekt der zentralen Trochlea femoris von 10 mm, DD posttraumatisch. V. a. Patella alta. Regelrechtes Femorotibialgelenk.
textInfo.originalDescription: Knie rechts: Fokaler umschriebener Knorpeldefekt der zentralen Trochlea femoris von 10 mm, DD posttraumatisch. V. a. Patella alta. Regelrechtes Femorotibialgelenk.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI shows a specific, localized area of cartilage damage measuring 10 millimeters in your right knee. This is located on the trochlea femoris, which is the groove on your thighbone where the kneecap glides. The radiologist notes this could be the result of a past injury. Additionally, your kneecap appears to be positioned slightly higher than normal, a condition called patella alta. The main weight-bearing part of your knee joint between the thighbone and shinbone appears normal.

textInfo.title: Slightly Elevated hs-CRP
textInfo.medicalDescription: CRP sensitiv ' 2.4 mg/l (<3). The result falls into the 'mittleres KHK Risiko' (medium coronary heart disease risk) category of 1.0-3.0 mg/l.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your high-sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) level is 2.4 mg/L. This level is within the range (1.0-3.0 mg/L) that suggests an average or intermediate risk of future cardiovascular events due to low-grade inflammation. Lifestyle modifications can help reduce inflammation.

textInfo.title: Increased Fat in Right Psoas Muscle
textInfo.medicalDescription: Right psoas major intramuscular fat (IMF) is elevated at 10.3% (normal range: 4.7% - 7.7%).
textInfo.originalDescription: biomarker_name: IMF_psoas_major right, value: 10.3, unit: %, minNormalValue: 4.7, maxNormalValue: 7.7
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The amount of fat within your right psoas major muscle, a key muscle in your lower back and hip, is higher than the optimal range. Elevated intramuscular fat can be associated with reduced muscle quality and function. The optimal range is between 4.7% and 7.7%, and your value is 10.3%.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin (berechnet): H 4.7 mmol/l (Ref: <3.9)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your non-HDL cholesterol level is 4.7 mmol/L, which is above the recommended level of less than 3.9 mmol/L. Non-HDL cholesterol is considered 'bad' cholesterol and high levels can contribute to artery plaque buildup.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Cholesterin ' H 6.2 mmol/l (<5)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is 6.2 mmol/L, which is higher than the recommended level of less than 5 mmol/L. High cholesterol can increase your risk of cardiovascular diseases. Lifestyle changes and, if necessary, medication can help manage this.

textInfo.title: Increased Fat in Left Psoas Muscle
textInfo.medicalDescription: Left psoas major intramuscular fat (IMF) is elevated at 9.1% (normal range: 5.3% - 8.3%).
textInfo.originalDescription: biomarker_name: IMF_psoas_major left, value: 9.1, unit: %, minNormalValue: 5.3, maxNormalValue: 8.3
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The amount of fat within your left psoas major muscle, a key muscle in your lower back and hip, is higher than the optimal range. Elevated intramuscular fat can be associated with reduced muscle quality and function. The optimal range is between 5.3% and 8.3%, and your value is 9.1%.

textInfo.title: Mildly Reduced Kidney Function (eGFR)
textInfo.medicalDescription: eGFR (Kreatinin, CKD-EPI) L 77 ml/min/1.73m2 (>90). This indicates Stage 2 Chronic Kidney Disease (mildly decreased GFR).
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR), a measure of kidney function, is 77 ml/min/1.73m2. This is below the normal value of over 90, indicating a mild reduction in kidney function (Stage 2 Chronic Kidney Disease). It's important to monitor this and discuss with your doctor how to protect your kidney health.

textInfo.title: Changes in the prostate, likely due to prior inflammation
textInfo.medicalDescription: Hypointense Veränderungen der peripheren Zone der Prostata bds. am ehesten postentzündlicher Genese bei fehlendem Nachweis einer Diffusionsrestriktion
textInfo.originalDescription: Hypointense Veränderungen der peripheren Zone der Prostata bds.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI of your prostate shows some areas in the outer part (peripheral zone) on both sides that appear darker than the surrounding tissue. The radiologist's interpretation is that these changes are most likely the result of previous inflammation. This conclusion is supported by the absence of a finding called 'diffusion restriction,' which can be a sign of more significant issues. Therefore, the observed changes are considered likely to be benign.

textInfo.title: Elevated LDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: LDL-Cholesterin ' H 4.5 mmol/l (<3.0)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your LDL ('bad') cholesterol level is 4.5 mmol/L, which is significantly higher than the standard upper limit of 3.0 mmol/L. High LDL cholesterol is a major risk factor for plaque buildup in your arteries, leading to heart disease and stroke.

textInfo.title: Elevated Non-HDL Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Non-HDL-Cholesterin (berechnet) H 4.7 mmol/l (<3.9)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your non-HDL cholesterol, which includes all 'bad' types of cholesterol, is 4.7 mmol/L. This is above the recommended limit of 3.9 mmol/L and indicates an increased risk for heart disease. Discussing management options with a doctor is advised.

textInfo.title: Mild age-appropriate degenerative changes in the mid and lower back
textInfo.medicalDescription: Leichte degenerative Veränderungen der BWS und LWS, altersentsprechend.
textInfo.originalDescription: Leichte degenerative Veränderungen der BWS und LWS, altersentsprechend.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: In your mid and lower back (thoracic and lumbar spine), the MRI reveals mild degenerative changes. The radiologist has noted that these findings of wear and tear are consistent with what is typically expected for your age.

textInfo.title: Increased Fat in Left Quadratus Lumborum Muscle
textInfo.medicalDescription: Left quadratus lumborum intramuscular fat (IMF) is 10.0%. While the provided normal range is 0%, lower values are considered optimal, and this value is elevated.
textInfo.originalDescription: biomarker_name: IMF_quadratus_lumborum left, value: 10, unit: %, minNormalValue: 0, maxNormalValue: 0
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Fat has been measured within your left quadratus lumborum muscle, a deep abdominal muscle in your lower back. Your value is 10.0%. While a specific normal range isn't provided for this measurement, lower levels of fat within muscles are generally considered better for muscle health and function.

textInfo.title: Elevated Total Cholesterol
textInfo.medicalDescription: Cholesterin: H 6.2 mmol/l (Ref: <5)
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your total cholesterol level is 6.2 mmol/L, which is higher than the recommended level of less than 5 mmol/L. High cholesterol can increase the risk of cardiovascular diseases.

textInfo.title: Highly Elevated Lipoprotein(a)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Lipoprotein (a): H 257 nmol/l (Ref: <75). Value is highly elevated, indicating a significant genetic risk factor for cardiovascular disease.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your Lipoprotein(a) level is 257 nmol/L, which is significantly higher than the normal range of less than 75 nmol/L. An elevated Lipoprotein(a) is a genetic risk factor for developing cardiovascular diseases like heart attacks and strokes, independent of cholesterol levels.

textInfo.title: Small bilateral inguinal hernias
textInfo.medicalDescription: Fetthaltige kleine Inguinalhernie links grösser rechts.
textInfo.originalDescription: Fetthaltige kleine Inguinalhernie links grösser rechts.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The scan identified small inguinal hernias on both sides of your groin, with the one on the left being larger than the one on the right. An inguinal hernia is a condition where tissue, in this case fat, pushes through a weak spot in the abdominal muscles. These are described as 'small' and 'fat-containing'.

textInfo.title: Increased Fat in Right Quadratus Lumborum Muscle
textInfo.medicalDescription: Right quadratus lumborum intramuscular fat (IMF) is 10.5%. While the provided normal range is 0%, lower values are considered optimal, and this value is elevated.
textInfo.originalDescription: biomarker_name: IMF_quadratus_lumborum right, value: 10.5, unit: %, minNormalValue: 0, maxNormalValue: 0
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Fat has been measured within your right quadratus lumborum muscle, a deep abdominal muscle in your lower back. Your value is 10.5%. While a specific normal range isn't provided for this measurement, lower levels of fat within muscles are generally considered better for muscle health and function.
